words.definition_en: existing in large quantities
words.example: Fish are abundant in the lake.

words.definition_en: enough, and often more than you need
words.definition_vi: dư dả, nhiều hơn mức cần thiết
words.example: There is ample time for discussion.

words.definition_en: a part of a place or building
words.example: The local area is very quiet.

words.definition_en: the amount, level, standard etc that is typical of a group
words.example: The average age of the students is 20.

words.definition_en: a number of people or things that are dealt with as a group
words.example: We processed a new batch of orders.

words.definition_en: the majority or large size/quantity of something
words.example: The bulk of the work is done.

words.definition_en: large in size, amount, or degree
words.example: We made considerable progress today.

words.definition_en: very many, too many to be counted
words.example: I've told him countless times.

words.definition_en: a measurement of something in a particular direction
words.example: A square has two dimensions.

words.definition_en: to become or make something become smaller or less important
words.example: Our resources are diminishing rapidly.

words.definition_en: a mathematical statement that two amounts are equal
words.example: Can you solve this equation?

words.definition_en: at the same distance from two places
words.example: The house is equidistant from the two cities.

words.definition_en: to become larger in size, number, or amount
words.example: The universe is expanding.

words.definition_en: the size or importance of something
words.example: We didn't realize the extent of the damage.

words.definition_en: having an end or a limit
words.example: The earth has finite resources.

words.definition_en: physical strength or power
words.definition_vi: lực lượng, sức mạnh, ép buộc
words.example: The force of the explosion was immense.

words.definition_en: a small part or amount of something
words.example: She only ate a fraction of her dinner.

words.definition_en: a large pile of something
words.example: There's a heap of clothes on the floor.

words.definition_en: a lack of balance between things
words.example: There is a trade imbalance between the two countries.

words.definition_en: extremely large
words.definition_vi: mênh mông, bao la, cực kỳ lớn
words.example: The cost of the project was immense.

words.definition_en: the strength or power of something
words.example: The intensity of the sun's rays was unbearable.

words.definition_en: the great size or importance of something
words.example: We realized the magnitude of the problem.

words.definition_en: very large, important, or serious
words.example: This is a major discovery.

words.definition_en: a large amount of a substance that has no particular shape
words.example: The sun is a mass of gas.

words.definition_en: very small in amount or number
words.example: He earns a meagre salary.

words.definition_en: not very large, important, or serious
words.example: It was only a minor accident.

words.definition_en: extremely small
words.example: The machine can detect minute amounts of gas.

words.definition_en: many, or involving many things
words.example: She suffered multiple injuries.

words.definition_en: a part of a number or amount
words.example: A large proportion of the students are from abroad.

words.definition_en: to measure or state the amount of something
words.example: It is difficult to quantify the risks.

words.definition_en: the speed or frequency of something
words.definition_vi: tỷ lệ, tốc độ, đánh giá
words.example: The inflation rate is rising.

words.definition_en: a relationship between two amounts showing how much bigger one is
words.example: The ratio of men to women was 2 to 1.

words.definition_en: a fixed amount of something that you are allowed to have
words.example: Petrol was rationed during the war.

words.definition_en: to become smaller in size
words.example: My shirt shrank in the wash.

words.definition_en: as much as is needed
words.example: We have sufficient evidence to convict him.

words.definition_en: an amount of money
words.example: A large sum of money was stolen.

words.definition_en: not smooth, flat, or level
words.example: The floor was uneven.

words.definition_en: extremely large
words.example: The vast majority of people agree.

words.definition_en: the amount of space something fills
words.definition_vi: thể tích, âm lượng, khối lượng
words.example: The volume of traffic is increasing.

words.definition_en: existing or happening in many places
words.example: There is widespread support for the new law.

words.definition_en: money given by the government to people who are poor, ill, or unemployed
words.example: He's on unemployment benefit.

words.definition_en: money paid to someone because they have suffered injury or loss
words.example: She received compensation for her injuries.

words.definition_en: money that a court orders someone to pay to someone else for harming them
words.definition_vi: tiền bồi thường thiệt hại (theo lệnh tòa)
words.example: The judge awarded damages of £5,000.

words.definition_en: an amount of money that you owe
words.example: I'm in debt to the bank.

words.definition_en: to take an amount from a total
words.example: Tax is deducted from your salary.

words.definition_en: to put money into a bank account
words.example: I deposited £100 in my account.

words.definition_en: an instruction to your bank to pay money directly to an organization
words.example: I pay my bills by direct debit.

words.definition_en: a part of a company's profit that is divided among shareholders
words.example: The company paid a high dividend this year.

words.definition_en: a first payment made when buying something expensive
words.definition_vi: tiền đặt cọc trả trước (khi mua trả góp)
words.example: We made a down payment on a new car.

words.definition_en: money provided by a bank or other institution to help start or run a business
words.example: They are looking for finance for their new project.

words.definition_en: an arrangement in which you pay a company and they pay your costs if you are ill/injured
words.example: Do you have health insurance?

words.definition_en: money that you must pay to a bank that has lent you money
words.example: The interest on the loan is 5%.

words.definition_en: money put into a business to make a profit
words.example: The project was a good investment.

words.definition_en: an amount of money paid at one time and not in small amounts
words.example: He received his pension as a lump sum.

words.definition_en: a legal arrangement by which you borrow money from a bank to buy a house
words.definition_vi: thế chấp, tiền vay mua nhà
words.example: We've just taken out a mortgage.

words.definition_en: an amount of money spent that is more than in your bank account
words.definition_vi: thanh toán quá số dư, thấu chi
words.example: I have a £500 overdraft.

words.definition_en: money paid regularly to someone who has retired
words.example: He lives on his state pension.

words.definition_en: one of the equal parts into which the ownership of a company is divided
words.example: The value of the shares fell.

words.definition_en: to buy shares or property hoping to make a profit when you sell them
words.example: He lost a lot of money speculating on the stock market.

words.definition_en: to take money out of a bank account
words.example: I'd like to withdraw £50, please.

words.definition_en: if separate amounts add up to a total, they form that total; combine to produce a result
words.definition_vi: tổng cộng là, dẫn đến kết quả
words.example: The small costs add up to a lot of money.

words.definition_en: divide something such as a total amount into separate parts
words.example: Let's break down the costs into monthly payments.

words.definition_en: increase or make something increase; gradually develop
words.definition_vi: tích lũy, xây dựng dần lên
words.example: She's building up a successful business.

words.definition_en: give someone money so that they do not act against you
words.example: They tried to buy off the witnesses.

words.definition_en: pay money to your business partner so that you can control all of a business
words.definition_vi: mua lại cổ phần (để nắm quyền kiểm soát)
words.example: The company was bought out by its management.

words.definition_en: buy large amounts of something or all of it that is available
words.example: Investors are buying up land in the area.

words.definition_en: take something from one year or period into the next one
words.example: You can carry over your unused holiday to next year.

words.definition_en: reach a particular number or amount
words.definition_vi: đạt được con số, ghi được (thành tích)
words.example: He has clocked up 100 goals for the team.

words.definition_en: if people club together, each gives some money to buy something
words.example: We clubbed together to buy her a present.

words.definition_en: try to make people support you or buy something
words.definition_vi: vận động, lôi kéo (sự ủng hộ, khách hàng)
words.example: He's trying to drum up some business.

words.definition_en: get much larger
words.example: The debts are beginning to mount up.

words.definition_en: give someone the same amount of money that you borrowed
words.example: I'll pay you back on Friday.

words.definition_en: spend or pay a lot of money; provide money from an investment
words.example: The insurance company paid out £10,000.

words.definition_en: think carefully and form an opinion about a person or situation
words.example: He sized up the situation before speaking.

words.definition_en: remove one number or quantity from another
words.example: Take 5 away from 12 and you get 7.

words.definition_en: make someone heavy or cause problems/worry for someone
words.example: She was weighed down with bags.

words.definition_en: a very small amount that will not have much effect
words.definition_vi: muối bỏ bể, giọt nước giữa đại dương
words.example: The money is just a drop in the ocean compared to what we need.

words.definition_en: neither make a profit nor lose money
words.example: We hope to break even in our first year of business.

words.definition_en: not reach a particular level or achieve something
words.definition_vi: không đạt được mục tiêu, thiếu hụt
words.example: The total fell short of our expectations.

words.definition_en: equal, or into two equal parts
words.definition_vi: năm mươi - năm mươi, chia đôi
words.example: We split the profits fifty-fifty.

words.definition_en: share the cost of something with someone so that you each pay 50%
words.example: Shall we go halves on the taxi?

words.word: it's as broad as it's long
words.definition_en: used for saying that you cannot choose between two things because they are equal
words.definition_vi: kẻ tám lạng người nửa cân, không khác gì nhau
words.example: Whether we go by train or car, it's as broad as it's long - it takes the same time.

words.definition_en: try to be as rich, successful, etc as your neighbours
words.definition_vi: đua đòi, luôn muốn bằng bạn bằng bè
words.example: They bought a new car just to keep up with the Joneses.

words.definition_en: very small, because you were very young
words.definition_vi: còn bé tí, khi còn nhỏ
words.example: I haven't seen him since I was knee-high to a grasshopper.

words.definition_en: obtain money, especially by acting dishonestly
words.definition_vi: trục lợi, làm giàu bất chính
words.example: He's just trying to line his own pockets.

words.definition_en: including every part of a particular thing
words.example: They sold the business lock, stock and barrel.

words.word: six of one (and) half a dozen of the other
words.definition_en: used for saying that two things are equally good or bad
words.definition_vi: như nhau cả thôi, tám lạng nửa cân
words.example: It's six of one and half a dozen of the other - both options are fine.

words.definition_en: large sum/amount
words.example: He made a tidy sum when he sold the house.

items.sentence: Thankfully, she only suffered ___ injuries in the accident.
items.explanation: 'Minor injuries' is a standard collocation meaning small and not serious.

items.sentence: A half, written as a/an ___ , looks like this: 1/2.
items.explanation: A fraction represents a part of a whole, such as 1/2.

items.sentence: In a few hundred years' time, oil will not be nearly so ___ here on Earth.
items.explanation: 'Abundant' means existing in large quantities, suitable for natural resources.

items.sentence: ___ is measured in cm3 or m3.
items.explanation: Volume is the amount of space occupied by an object, measured in cubic units.

items.sentence: Don't wash that sweater in really hot water or it'll ___ .
items.explanation: Clothes 'shrink' (become smaller) when washed in hot water.

items.sentence: Maps on paper can only show the world in two ___ .
items.explanation: Paper is a flat surface, so it has two dimensions (length and width).

items.sentence: Why are all your clothes in a ___ on the floor?
items.explanation: A 'heap' is an untidy pile of things.

items.sentence: Sending out e-mails that people haven't asked for to ___ addresses is often known as 'spamming'.
items.explanation: 'Multiple' means many or various; spam involves sending to many addresses.

exercises.label: B
exercises.instruction: Each of the words in bold is in the wrong sentence. Write the correct words on the lines.

items.sentence: The ___ (uneven) age of everyone in this class is 16.5 .
items.explanation: 'Average age' refers to the mean age of a group.

items.sentence: It's impossible to ___ (expand) how much destruction the hurricane has caused .
items.explanation: 'Quantify' means to measure or state the amount of something.

items.sentence: There was a severe shortage of food in the Second World War so many governments had to ___ (force) it.
items.explanation: 'Ration' means to limit the distribution of something scarce.

items.sentence: Balloons ___ (ration) when you blow into them .
items.explanation: Balloons get larger (expand) when filled with air.

items.sentence: There isn't a/an ___ (equidistant) number of numbers because you can always add one more onto the biggest number you can think of .
items.explanation: A 'finite' number is limited; numbers are infinite.

items.sentence: Russia is an absolutely ___ (finite) country - it can take two weeks to travel from one side to the other by train.
items.explanation: 'Vast' means extremely large in area.

items.sentence: Any point on a circle is ___ (vast) from the centre .
items.explanation: By definition, all points on a circle's circumference are the same distance (equidistant) from the center.

items.sentence: There's a huge ___ (quantify) between what the rich and the poorest members of society earn .
items.explanation: 'Imbalance' refers to an unequal distribution or difference.

items.sentence: The ___ (imbalance) that makes things drop to the ground is called gravity .
items.explanation: Gravity is a natural 'force'.

items.sentence: The walls of the old cottage were rough and ___ (average) in height.
items.explanation: 'Uneven' means not smooth or level.

items.sentence: The density of an object is calculated by dividing its ___ (intensity) by its volume .
items.explanation: Scientific formula: Density = Mass / Volume.

items.sentence: The storm hit the coast with such ___ (mass) that many buildings in the region were destroyed .
items.explanation: 'Intensity' refers to the strength or power of a storm.

exercises.label: C
exercises.instruction: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: The judge awarded us 1,000 euros ___ (damage/ damages) when the builder admitted he hadn't followed safety standards.
items.explanation: 'Damages' (plural) is the legal term for money awarded by a court.

items.sentence: The hotel was so bad I'm thinking of asking for ___ (compensation / debit) from the travel agency.
items.explanation: Compensation is money paid to make up for poor service or loss.

items.sentence: Many companies ___ (withdraw/ deduct) tax from your salary before you get paid.
items.explanation: 'Deduct' means to take away an amount from a total (like tax from salary).

items.sentence: Hello. I'd like to ___ (withdraw/ deduct) 500 euros from my account, please.
items.explanation: 'Withdraw' is the standard term for taking money out of a bank account.

items.sentence: You can pay in 12 monthly instalments, but you'll have to make an initial ___ (lump sum/ down payment) of £25.
items.explanation: A 'down payment' is the first part of a payment made when buying something on credit.

items.sentence: If you win, you can choose to receive it all in one go as a ___ (lump sum/ down payment) or you can have a certain amount per year for life.
items.explanation: A 'lump sum' is a single large payment instead of several smaller ones.

items.sentence: I'll ___ (finance/ deposit) the money in your bank account in the morning.
items.explanation: 'Deposit' means to put money into a bank account.

items.sentence: I'm thinking of ___ (speculating/ depositing) on the stock exchange. Do you think it's risky?
items.explanation: 'Speculating' involves buying assets (like shares) hoping to make a profit despite the risk.

items.sentence: Why don't you arrange to pay your bills by direct ___ (debt/ debit)? That way, they're automatically paid from your account.
items.explanation: 'Direct debit' is a specific banking arrangement.

items.sentence: Where is the company planning to get the ___ (sum / finance) for this project from?
items.explanation: 'Finance' refers to the funding needed for a business or project.

exercises.label: D
exercises.instruction: Write a word from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: Different bank accounts pay different amounts of ___ .
items.explanation: Banks pay interest on the money you keep in an account.

items.sentence: If your salary doesn't always get you to the end of the month, you'll have to arrange a/an ___ with your bank.
items.explanation: An 'overdraft' allows you to spend more money than you have in your account.

items.sentence: If you're planning to buy a house, you'll probably need to get a/an ___ .
items.explanation: A 'mortgage' is a loan specifically for buying property.

items.sentence: If you're thinking about taking out life ___ , make sure you talk to an independent financial advisor.
items.explanation: Life insurance provides financial protection in case of death.

items.sentence: Make sure you know what ___ you're entitled to. If you're on a low income... you may receive [money] from the state.
items.explanation: 'Benefits' are payments from the government to those in need.

items.sentence: Start paying into a/an ___ scheme now to ensure you have a comfortable retirement.
items.explanation: A 'pension' is a fund set up for retirement.

items.sentence: It goes without saying that you should try to avoid getting into too much ___ .
items.explanation: 'Debt' is money that you owe to others.

items.sentence: If you do manage to start saving a little each month, you might want to think about buying some ___ .
items.explanation: 'Shares' are units of ownership in a company.

items.sentence: Their value can go down of course, but they can be a good ___ , particularly in the mid- to long-term.
items.explanation: Buying shares is a form of investment.

items.sentence: Many companies pay an annual ___ to shareholders.
items.explanation: A 'dividend' is a portion of a company's profit paid to shareholders.

exercises.label: E
exercises.instruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: If your debts are mounting ___ and your financial problems are weighing you...
items.explanation: 'Mount up' means to increase in size or amount.

items.sentence: ...your financial problems are weighing you ___ , the first thing to do is to stay calm.
items.explanation: 'Weigh down' means to cause someone worry or problems.

items.sentence: Sizing ___ your financial situation - however painful - is far better than just ignoring it.
items.explanation: 'Size up' means to assess or form an opinion about a situation.

items.sentence: Break these outgoings ___ into essentials (such as electricity bills and food) and luxuries.
items.explanation: 'Break down' means to divide a total into smaller parts.

items.sentence: ...and take your essential outgoings ___ from your income.
items.explanation: 'Take away' means to subtract.

items.sentence: The amount that's remaining is the amount you can afford to pay ___ to your creditors each month.
items.explanation: 'Pay back' means to repay money owed.

items.sentence: ...they know that if you - and they - just let the debt build ___ indefinitely it will never be paid off.
items.explanation: 'Build up' means to increase gradually.

items.sentence: If your essential expenses add up ___ more than your income, then your situation is more serious.
items.explanation: 'Add up to' means to total a certain amount.

items.sentence: ...reduce the amount of money you have to pay ___ each month on essentials.
items.explanation: 'Pay out' means to spend or distribute money.

items.sentence: The big supermarket chains have bought ___
items.answer: up most of the land round here already.
items.explanation: 'Buy up' means to buy all or a large amount of something.

items.sentence: If we club ___
items.answer: together, I'm sure we can afford to get Mrs Timms a nice leaving present.
items.explanation: 'Club together' means to pool money with others for a common purpose.

items.sentence: Phil's clocked ___
items.answer: up over 700 hours of flying time already.
items.explanation: 'Clock up' means to reach or record a particular number.

items.sentence: One good way to drum ___
items.answer: up support is to get people in the high street to sign a petition.
items.explanation: 'Drum up' means to try to obtain support or business.

items.sentence: Six managers bought ___
items.answer: out the company when it was threatened with closure.
items.explanation: 'Buy out' means to pay someone to take over their share of a business.

items.sentence: I've got two weeks' holiday left but I think I'll carry ___
items.answer: it over till next year and take it in January.
items.explanation: 'Carry over' means to take something from one period into the next.

items.sentence: I'd never buy ___
items.answer: blackmailers off - I'd rather go to the police and face the consequences.
items.explanation: 'Buy off' means to bribe someone to stop them from acting against you.

exercises.label: G
exercises.instruction: Phrasal verbs with together, such as club together, often contain the idea of people doing something as a group. Tick the sentences which contain that idea.

items.sentence: Eight countries in the region have banded together to make a free-trade zone .
items.explanation: 'Band together' involves a group of people/entities acting as one.

items.sentence: I didn't know anyone was coming to dinner, so I had to cobble a meal together from whatever was in the fridge .
items.explanation: 'Cobble together' means to put something together quickly/clumsily, not necessarily as a group.

items.sentence: Why don't we all get together on Tuesday evening?
items.explanation: 'Get together' means to meet as a group.

items.sentence: The twins have been behaving so badly recently. I should knock their heads together!
items.explanation: This is an idiom for punishing people, not people acting together as a group.

items.sentence: Everyone in the village pulled together to make the carnival a success .
items.explanation: 'Pull together' means to work together as a team.

items.sentence: The only way we'll all get off this mountain alive is if we stick together .
items.explanation: 'Stick together' means to stay with each other as a group.

items.sentence: The three of us put together quite an interesting proposal.
items.explanation: 'Put together' here implies a collaborative effort by three people.

items.sentence: It took quite a while but at ___ (slow / long) last the website's starting to take off.
items.explanation: 'At long last' is a fixed idiom meaning finally after a long time.

items.sentence: Did you really offer Liz a 50 per cent ___ (share / piece) in the business?
items.explanation: A 'share in a business' refers to the portion of ownership.

items.sentence: No, you can't borrow a thousand euros! I'm not made ___ (of / with) money, you know!
items.explanation: 'Made of money' is a common idiom meaning very rich.

items.sentence: Nice apartments are a bit ___ (thin / slim) on the ground round here.
items.explanation: 'Thin on the ground' is an idiom meaning scarce or few in number.

items.sentence: I literally went weak ___ (on / at) the knees when he told me how much he'd won.
items.explanation: 'Weak at the knees' is a fixed idiom describing a physical reaction to strong emotion.

items.sentence: Let's discuss this ___ (in / with) depth tomorrow.
items.explanation: 'In depth' is the correct prepositional phrase meaning thoroughly.

items.sentence: The field's just over four hectares ___ (at / in) size.
items.explanation: 'In size' is the standard phrase for describing dimensions.

items.sentence: They said on the news the escaped convict's still ___ (at / in) large.
items.explanation: 'At large' is a fixed phrase meaning free, not captured.

items.sentence: The problem with Jimmy is that he's such a ___ (weak / poor) loser.
items.explanation: A 'poor loser' is someone who gets angry or upset when they lose.

items.sentence: I hate landlords who grow ___ (fat / large) on the rent they collect.
items.explanation: 'Grow fat on' is an idiom meaning to become rich because of something, often at the expense of others.

items.sentence: The area used to be very ___ (rich / wealthy) in tin and copper.
items.explanation: 'Rich in' means having a lot of a particular resource.

items.sentence: It's a good business opportunity ___ (for / with) my money.
items.explanation: 'For my money' is an idiom meaning 'in my opinion'.

items.sentence: There were a whole ___ (number / load) of people I knew at the opening.
items.explanation: 'A whole load of' is an informal phrase meaning many.

exercises.label: I
exercises.instruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Jane just stood there and watched while we did all the work. A ___ lot of use that was!
items.explanation: 'A fat lot of use' is an ironic idiom meaning not useful at all.

items.sentence: You can make a lot of money if you work in ___ finance.
items.explanation: 'High finance' refers to the activity of lending/investing very large sums of money.

items.sentence: We've travelled the length and ___ of the country looking for the next Teen Star!
items.explanation: 'The length and breadth of' is a fixed phrase meaning everywhere in a place.

items.sentence: Hi Chloe! Long time no ___ !
items.explanation: 'Long time no see' is a fixed expression used when meeting someone after a long time.

items.sentence: The first moments of the universe are often known as the Big ___ .
items.explanation: The 'Big Bang' theory of the origin of the universe.

items.sentence: It only cost five euros and we were there all day. We really did get our money's ___ .
items.explanation: 'Get your money's worth' means to get good value for the money you spend.

items.sentence: It's a pay and ___ car park, so get a ticket from the machine before you go shopping.
items.explanation: 'Pay and display' is a system where you buy a ticket and show it in your car window.

items.sentence: 'Share and share ___ , that's what I say.'
items.explanation: 'Share and share alike' means that everyone should have an equal share.

items.sentence: And suddenly Diana appeared out of ___ air.
items.explanation: 'Out of thin air' means suddenly and unexpectedly.

items.sentence: I think you're on very weak ___ with that argument.
items.explanation: 'On weak ground' means that your argument or position is not supported by facts.

exercises.label: J
exercises.instruction: For each question, write one word which can be used in all three sentences.

items.sentence: I think we're running ___ of petrol. / Nick has got such a ___ temper. / ...at such ___ notice.
items.explanation: 'Running short', 'short temper', and 'short notice' all use the word 'short'.

items.sentence: It ___ to shop around... / ...as long as he ___ his way. / ...when someone ___ me a compliment.
items.explanation: 'It pays to', 'pays his way', and 'pays a compliment' all use the word 'pay/pays'.

items.sentence: Maybe that's your ___ . / ...my ___ in life... / ...got a ___ on this weekend...
items.explanation: 'That's your lot', 'lot in life', and 'have a lot on' all use the word 'lot'.

items.sentence: Pauline seems to be in very ___ spirits... / ...quite a ___ risk... / It's ___ time you started...
items.explanation: 'High spirits', 'high risk', and 'high time' all use the word 'high'.

items.sentence: Tom's actually quite a ___ name... / ...make such a ___ thing out of it. / ...make it ___ in the entertainment business.
items.explanation: 'Big name', 'big thing', and 'make it big' all use the word 'big'.

items.sentence: I don't think I've got any ___ change... / You made me feel so ___ ... / ...keen on making ___ talk at parties.
items.explanation: 'Small change', 'feel small', and 'small talk' all use the word 'small'.

exercises.label: K
exercises.instruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: Some people will do anything to make money. -> Some people will ___ to make money.
items.explanation: 'Go to great lengths' means to do anything necessary to achieve something.

items.sentence: I don't know why you're saying this is so important. -> I don't know why you're ___ this.
items.answer: making such a big deal out of
items.explanation: 'Make a big deal out of' means to treat something as very important.

items.sentence: Simon said he had no idea what he was doing for the first few weeks of his new job. -> Simon said he ___ for the first few weeks of his new job.
items.explanation: 'Out of your depth' means in a situation that is too difficult for you.

items.sentence: I think they're largely responsible for what's happened. -> I hold them ___ responsible for what's happened.
items.explanation: 'In no small way' is a formal way of saying 'largely' or 'significantly'.

items.sentence: We've searched everywhere for the lottery ticket and can't find it anywhere. -> We've searched ___ for the lottery ticket and can't find it anywhere.
items.explanation: 'Search high and low' means to search everywhere.

items.sentence: We talked about investments for a very long time. -> We talked ___ about investments.
items.explanation: 'At (great) length' means for a long time and in detail.

exercises.label: L
exercises.instruction: Each of the words or phrases in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: I was hoping I'd raise about 1,000 euros for the local hospital, but I fell ___ (small) by a few hundred euros.
items.explanation: 'Fall short' means to fail to reach an amount or standard.

items.sentence: Jerry and I agreed to split the costs ___ (twenty-twenty) so we'd each pay exactly half .
items.explanation: Splitting 'fifty-fifty' means dividing something into two equal halves.

items.sentence: There are two ways to get to Innsbruck, but I think it's as ___ (wide) as it's long .
items.explanation: 'As broad as it's long' means that two choices are equal.

items.sentence: My dad taught me about the importance of saving money when I was knee-high to a ___ (locust).
items.explanation: 'Knee-high to a grasshopper' is a common idiom meaning very young.

items.sentence: So many politicians just seem to be trying to line their own ___ (wallets) these days - it's disgraceful.
items.explanation: 'Line your own pockets' means to make yourself rich, usually dishonestly.

items.sentence: The cost is a drop in the ___ (sea) in terms of the whole marketing budget.
items.explanation: The idiom is 'a drop in the ocean', meaning a tiny, insignificant amount.

items.sentence: I've decided to sell the whole business - lock, stock and ___ (trigger) .
items.explanation: 'Lock, stock and barrel' means including everything, derived from parts of a gun.

items.sentence: Don't pay the whole bill. Let's go ___ (half) .
items.explanation: The idiom for sharing a cost is 'go halves'.

items.sentence: There are two ways to get to Innsbruck, but I think it's six of one and half a ___ (twelve) of the other.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'six of one and half a dozen of the other'.

items.sentence: Lucy managed to save quite a ___ (clean) sum last summer .
items.explanation: A 'tidy sum' is an idiom for a large amount of money.

items.sentence: We're not going to make a profit this year, but we should just break ___ (equal) .
items.explanation: 'Break even' means to reach a point where income equals expenses.

items.sentence: He's just trying to ___ (stay) up with the Joneses, if you ask me.
items.explanation: The idiom is 'keep up with the Joneses'.

exercises.label: M
exercises.instruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: How dare they ___ blame without knowing all the facts first? (PORTION)
items.explanation: 'Apportion' means to distribute or assign (blame, responsibility).

items.sentence: At the ___ of summer, the temperature can reach 50C. (HIGH)
items.explanation: 'Height' is the noun form of high, used here for 'peak'.

items.sentence: I'd like to make a ___ from my bank account, please. (DRAW)
items.explanation: 'Withdrawal' is the noun form of withdraw.

items.sentence: Doris Carter... puts her ___ down to having a loving family... (LONG)
items.explanation: 'Longevity' means long life.

items.sentence: A ___ of residents now support the proposal... (MAJOR)
items.explanation: 'Majority' is the noun form meaning most people.

items.sentence: What strength ___ do those binoculars have? (MAGNIFY)
items.explanation: 'Magnification' is the noun form for the power of making things look larger.

items.sentence: We took out a loan... so we're going to have to make monthly ___ for the next five years. (PAY)
items.explanation: 'Payments' is the plural noun for money paid.

items.sentence: Liskeard and Callington are basically ___ from here... (DISTANT)
items.explanation: 'Equidistant' means at the same distance.

items.sentence: Their help was hugely ___ . (BENEFIT)
items.explanation: 'Beneficial' is the adjective form of benefit.

items.sentence: Please keep e-mails short. ___ makes everyone's lives easier. (BRIEF)
items.explanation: 'Brevity' is the noun form meaning the quality of being brief.

items.sentence: The school's football pitch has been ___ over the summer... (LONG)
items.explanation: 'Lengthened' is the verb form meaning made longer.

items.sentence: I don't want to ___ the agony for you... (LONG)
items.explanation: 'Prolong' means to make something last longer.

items.sentence: You might find it hard to answer all the questions in the ___ time. (LOT)
items.explanation: 'Allotted' is the adjective form meaning given or assigned.

exercises.label: N
exercises.instruction: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.

items.sentence: Albert Einstein's work has enormously ___ our understanding... (BROAD)
items.explanation: Verb form 'broadened' means made wider/more extensive.

items.sentence: ...and has had a ___ impact on all our lives. (CONSIDER)
items.explanation: Adjective form 'considerable' means great or significant.

items.sentence: To a very great ___ , it is Einstein who is responsible... (EXTEND)
items.explanation: Noun form 'extent' used in the phrase 'to a great extent'.

items.sentence: ___ , without E=mc2, we would not have nuclear power... (ADD)
items.explanation: Adverbial connector used to add information.

items.sentence: At the time, his paper was ___ ignored... (LARGE)
items.explanation: Adverb meaning mostly or to a great degree.

items.sentence: ...which was an ___ of his 1905 paper... (EXTEND)
items.explanation: Noun form meaning a development that includes more things.

items.sentence: The idea that only a tiny ___ of scientists... (MINOR)
items.explanation: Noun form meaning a small part of a larger group.

items.sentence: As he was clearly out of his ___ ... (DEEP)
items.explanation: In the idiom 'out of one's depth'.

items.sentence: Space, time, energy and mass are ___ subjects... (WEIGH)
items.explanation: Adjective meaning serious and important.

items.sentence: The implications... may well be ___ . (FINITE)
items.explanation: Adjective meaning without end or limit.

exercises.label: O
exercises.instruction: The prefix en-, as in enlarge, can be put on some adjectives, nouns and verbs to make verbs. Write these words, with en-, in the correct category.

items.sentence: put in/on (a) ...
items.answer: encircle, endanger, enlist, enthrone, entomb, entrap
items.explanation: These verbs mean putting someone/something in a circle, danger, a list, on a throne, in a tomb, or in a trap.

items.sentence: make ...
items.explanation: These verbs mean to make something/someone able, dear, large, rich, or a slave.

items.sentence: bring into effect
items.explanation: These verbs mean to put into action/law, code, force, or a state of rage.

exercises.label: P
exercises.instruction: Write a word with en- from exercise O in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: Are you really thinking of ___ in the army?
items.explanation: 'Enlist' means to join the armed forces.

items.sentence: This new software should ___ us to speed up the distribution process no end.
items.explanation: 'Enable' means to make it possible for someone to do something.

items.sentence: There's no point having a law if the police can't ___ it.
items.explanation: 'Enforce' means to make sure that people obey a law or rule.

items.sentence: We were completely ___ by journalists and photographers and couldn't get to the car.
items.explanation: 'Encircle' means to surround someone or something completely.

items.sentence: The destruction of the rainforests is ___ the survival of a number of species.
items.explanation: 'Endanger' means to put someone or something in a situation where they might be harmed.

items.sentence: Can we ___ the photo so we can see their faces in a bit more detail?
items.explanation: 'Enlarge' means to make something bigger.
